# Questions in Livestream 130

Live Stream #130: Million Dollar Questions

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=652" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:52</a> | Did the Great Queen take precedence over the firstborn of a secondary wife in Egyptian succession? | Yes; the son of the Great Queen normally has the stronger claim to inherit the throne. | He says Egyptian royal inheritance was not simply ordinal birth order. The heir designate was normally the son born to the Great Queen, or to the queen later made Great Queen, so that son inherited the throne regardless of other sons born earlier to secondary wives. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:25</a> | Have you seen the 2016 anime Erased? | Yes; he calls it a heavy anime with dark themes including child abuse and murder. | He has seen it and describes it as excellent but emotionally heavy. He notes its dark themes, including abuse and serial murder, and says some scenes depend on Japanese cultural context, such as the significance of bacon and eggs as a luxury breakfast for an abused child. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=964" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:04</a> | How did the Israelites worship while enslaved in Egypt? | He says they worshiped pagan Canaanite deities, including figures such as Rephaim and Astarte. | He says the Israelites in Egypt worshiped pagan Canaanite gods, especially Baal and figures such as Astarte, in a temple setting at Avaris. He describes their worship as Egyptian in style, with Egyptian priestly customs, incense rituals, shaved priests, and gods other than Yahweh as the objects of worship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=1517" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:17</a> | Are there any apologetics programs you actually recommend? | He recommends Southern Evangelical Seminary. | He recommends Southern Evangelical Seminary as a program he has heard good things about, while noting its Thomistic orientation and warning Protestant viewers that some students convert to Catholicism. He also mentions Dallas Theological Seminary and formerly Trinity Evangelical Divinity School, then says a person could approximate apologetics training by majoring in church history and taking substantial philosophy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=1902" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:42</a> | Does Romans 5:12 preclude pre-fall human death? | He reads the passage as focused on death through sin in Adam, not as a simple pre-fall animal or human death prooftext. | He argues Romans 5:12 does not automatically rule out all pre-fall physical death because the ancient Near Eastern concept of death includes separation, not only bodily death. He frames the fall as separation from God and sacred space, while noting that some biological death, such as plant cells being eaten, would have had to occur before the fall. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=2225" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:05</a> | How would biblical authors have defined the term human? | He says the question is difficult because biblical categories are not modern biological taxonomy. | He answers through biblical anthropology and the image of God in Genesis 1. Humanity is defined by being God's image in his temple, not by a checklist of abilities; people are most human in the ideal sense when they reflect God's goodness, mercy, kindness, moral accountability, and the likeness God recognizes in them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=2577" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:57</a> | Which is more fun to visit, Egypt or Israel? | He is biased toward Egypt because he has been there many times and has not been to Israel. | He openly says he is biased because he has visited Egypt many times and has not been to Israel. He praises Egypt's evening markets, haggling culture, temples, mastabas, and restaurants when chosen well, and says Egypt probably has more to see and may be more fun, though either place can broaden a visitor's worldview. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=2860" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:40</a> | Do Jeremiah 7:22 and Amos 5:25 saying Israel did not sacrifice in the wilderness prove the prophets lacked the Pentateuch? | No; he says that reading misses the point, with Amos 5:25 clearer than Jeremiah 7:22. | He says the passages are about obedience and idolatry, not evidence that the prophets lacked the Pentateuch. Grain offerings were not required in the wilderness because Israel lacked settled agriculture and reliable grain supplies; the prophets' criticism is that Israel kept its idols and failed to listen to God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=3411" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:51</a> | What does Nahum 2:2 mean by God restoring the majesty of Jacob as well as Israel? | He treats Jacob and Israel as functioning in parallel in the poetic couplet. | He explains Nahum 2:2 as poetry built around parallel couplets. Jacob and Israel are synonymous repetitions that fill out one line, then contrast with devastation and destroyed vine branches in the next; the poetic contrast heightens the message that God will restore what devastators destroyed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=3652" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:52</a> | What are your biggest concerns with Reformed theology, specifically the Five Points of Dort? | He says he is not deeply familiar with Dort and gives a cautious answer rather than a full critique. | He narrows the answer to common Calvinist points because he has not recently read Dort in depth. He questions unconditional election as arbitrary if God has no reasons, distinguishes election from salvation, accepts total depravity, rejects irresistible grace as stated, and says perseverance is better understood as God keeping those who remain on the path rather than preserving someone who rejects election. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=4185" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:45</a> | Does Reformed theology have a balanced view of God's sovereignty and human will, especially libertarian versus compatibilist will? | He discusses branches of Calvinism and says the balance depends on how those categories are handled. | He distinguishes libertarian Reformed views, especially as he reads Westminster's primary and secondary causes, from compatibilist views. Both can be balanced, but he leans toward the Westminster-style account where God sets sovereign boundaries while human beings act as real secondary causes under that sovereignty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=4535" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:35</a> | Does Daniel 12:3 connect resurrection with stars because resurrected people become angelic beings? | No; he says the text uses shining or glory imagery and does not require becoming angels. | He reads Daniel 12:2-3 as teaching glorified resurrection, not transformation into angels. Since stars can function as angelic imagery, the righteous are described as shining like angelic beings, especially those who lead many to righteousness, but they do not become angels. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=4818" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:18</a> | Would you do a livestream with David Wood to explain your view of eternal punishment? | Yes, he says he would be willing to do that. | He says he would gladly do it if David Wood contacted him, partly because he respects Wood's work and thinks the topic of eternal punishment should be discussed. He expects such a conversation would be in good faith and says he would relish the opportunity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=5603" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:23</a> | In Heiser's Deuteronomy 32 worldview, did Eastern religions develop their idols and ideas about God through the gods that led them? | He treats the question as related to divine-council theology and the nations, but not as a simple one-to-one origin story. | He says Heiser's model is strongly influenced by Second Temple divine-council ideas and does not map cleanly onto Egypt. Egyptian religion, in his account, developed from animism to local monotheisms and then aggregated into polytheism as Egypt nationalized, while idolatry itself arises from fallen human nature even apart from gods leading people astray. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=6562" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:22</a> | What are the heavenly bodies in 1 Corinthians 15:40? | He reads the passage by comparing earthly and heavenly kinds of glory in 1 Corinthians 15. | He says Paul first refers to celestial bodies such as the sun, moon, and stars, then pivots from that accepted comparison to resurrected bodies. The argument contrasts earthly and heavenly glory, with stars also carrying angelic associations, so Paul uses the imagery to explain the differing glory of the resurrection body. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=6901" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:01</a> | What are your thoughts on Molinism? | He says he is not very familiar with Molinism and cannot give a strong evaluation. | He says he needs to read more before forming a firm opinion. He already accepts middle knowledge as a philosophical principle, but he does not think accepting middle knowledge alone makes someone a Molinist, and he reserves judgment until he has done the necessary reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=7401" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:21</a> | Did judges judge priests, or was it only God? | Judges sometimes acted like a supreme court, so it was not only direct divine judgment. | He says Israel's judges were not only temporary military deliverers but also handled many legal disputes. They could judge religious disputes, priestly disagreements, taxation, divorce, property distribution, false prophets, and other high-level legal matters, functioning like a supreme court under God's authority. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=7655" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:35</a> | What does modalism deny God of, and what are your best arguments against it? | Modalism collapses the three persons into one role-switching person; he argues Scripture shows Father, Son, and Spirit as distinct persons. | He defines modalism as one divine person switching roles as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. He argues against it from scenes where the three appear distinctly, such as Christ's baptism and the Transfiguration, from passages like Isaiah 44:6 that distinguish the Lord and his Redeemer, and from resurrection texts that attribute Christ's resurrection to Father, Son, and Spirit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=7965" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:45</a> | How would you answer Oneness Pentecostals who say the term person is not in the Bible? | The term is a later vocabulary label, but Scripture portrays the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit personally. | He says absence of the vocabulary does not disprove the reality it names. Acts 5 treats the Holy Spirit as someone who can be lied to, and Acts repeatedly shows the Spirit guiding, teaching, enlightening, filling, and being grieved, which are personal actions even if the later term person is not used. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=8500" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:40</a> | What about the argument that Jesus never existed because there was no J in Greek? | He rejects it as a bad linguistic argument; Jesus is the English form that comes through Greek and Latin from Hebrew/Aramaic forms. | He calls the argument unserious because names change form across languages. Greek Iesous is the Greek form of Hebrew or Aramaic Joshua/Yehoshua, and English Jesus comes through Greek and Latin; the lack of the English letter J in Greek or Hebrew says nothing about whether Jesus existed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=8722" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:22</a> | What are your thoughts on The Chosen using poetic creative license with the Bible? | Creative license is inevitable in visual adaptation, but it becomes a problem when it reads ideas into the text. | He says some creative license is unavoidable when adapting written biblical narrative into visual media, because day-to-day details have to be supplied. The danger is crossing from responsible adaptation into eisegesis, especially when viewers treat creative additions as Scripture; creative license should support biblical truth rather than oppose it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=9276" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:36</a> | Do you prefer The Hobbit or The Lord of the Rings, and is Warhammer heretical? | He prefers The Lord of the Rings and does not treat Warhammer as heretical in itself. | He says he prefers The Lord of the Rings over The Hobbit and also likes the wider prehistory. On war imagery, he says biblical war psalms are not heretical because Scripture itself portrays Yahweh as a warrior, defender, judge, and opponent of sin, with imagery such as the mighty hand and outstretched arm. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=9505" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:38:25</a> | What is the biblical evidence for Adam and Eve as historical individuals? | He says there is no direct external evidence, but Scripture treats them as real progenitor figures in a mytho-historical narrative. | He says there is no direct external evidence for Adam and Eve and that the Genesis 1-3 material is mytho-history: a historical core wrapped in theological and temple imagery. Biblically, Adam and Eve are treated as real progenitor figures or clan identities, but because they belong before recorded history, corroborating historical evidence should not be expected. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=10017" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:57</a> | Is the name Israel a combination of Isis, Ra, and El? | No; he says Israel is Semitic and the Isis/Ra/El claim fails linguistically. | He rejects the Isis-Ra-El claim as linguistic nonsense. Israel is Semitic, with El as the only remotely relevant element, while Isis is Egyptian Eset and Ra is better rendered Re; the Egyptian and Hebrew forms do not match, and the name means something like struggling or fighting with El. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=10436" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:56</a> | What is the best argument atheists make against Christianity? | He says the strongest "argument" is usually not reason but desire: not wanting to live under God's righteousness. | He says he does not think atheists generally make strong rational arguments against Christianity. In his view, their most compelling argument is a lifestyle or desire argument: they do not want to submit to God, want to make their own rules, or object to God's moral authority, with later reasoning often used to support a prior choice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=10619" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:59</a> | Have you read Jewish arguments against Christianity and for Jesus not being the Messiah? | Yes; he says Jewish apologetics against Christianity is generally very weak. | He says he has read and encountered rabbinic Jewish arguments against Christianity and finds them weak. He characterizes many as emotional appeals about betraying Jewish heritage or as claims about Jesus' messianic credentials that Christian writers have answered historically, while noting that cultural religions often use apologetics to preserve community boundaries. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=11322" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:42</a> | What is the psychology of apostasy? | He says apostasy often begins with failure to grow in discipleship, leaving people vulnerable to falling away. | He defines apostasy as falling away after embracing the faith and says it often begins with lack of growth. He connects it to the parable of the sower, Hebrews 6, weak discipleship, black-and-white hermeneutics, and life-stage pressures, arguing that churches must make disciplined followers through teaching rather than assuming attendance equals discipleship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=12225" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:45</a> | Is it true that Jews once believed in the two powers based on Daniel 7:13-14? | Yes; he says two-powers theology existed in Second Temple Judaism and is found in Daniel and other texts. | He says two-powers theology is found in Second Temple Jewish literature and is connected not only to Daniel 7:13-14 but also to Genesis 1 and texts such as Isaiah 44:6. He adds that not all Jews held it then or now, but some Jewish traditions still preserve versions of a divine duality short of Christian Trinitarianism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=12557" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:17</a> | How much controversy is there in Christianity over the identity of the Angel of the Lord, and what is the significance of him going up with the offering in Judges 13:20? | He treats Judges 13 as important for identifying the Angel of the Lord and then discusses broader Christian disagreement. | He says the angel ascending in the altar flame signals a theophany rather than an ordinary angelic visitation, because the offering is made to God and an angel would not enter the divine altar flame. The controversy is whether a given Angel of the Lord appearance is a christophany or another theophany, and he says some cases remain uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=12918" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:35:18</a> | If testimony is an epistemological standard, can resurrection-magic testimonies cast doubt on the Resurrection? | He says that would require resurrection magic to be demonstrated, not merely asserted by testimony. | He rejects testimony as a complete epistemological standard, though it can count as evidence. Claims of resurrection magic would carry their own evidential burden, including whether the testimony is reliable and whether the practice is efficacious, and even successful evidence for such magic would not by itself disprove Jesus' resurrection or divinity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/9nYsP-vzSXQ?t=14040" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:00</a> | Will you play guitar for us? | No; he says he has standards and will not play guitar on the stream. | He declines because he lives in an apartment with thin walls, uses guitar mainly as a composition tool rather than for live performance, and does not want to risk noise problems with neighbors or the landlord. He notes that viewers have heard his compositions in the channel music, but he will not play guitar live on stream. |
